sentences.text: Yeah.
Actually at that point it was called a home.
It was not a true orphanage.
But I must go back to the platform at the train station in Paris.
When, when this transport arrived, I saw my father there, even though I was told that he had to be somewhere in southern France to take his ship.
But he was at the train station.
And a cousin, my mother's cousin was there as well.
And it felt good for me to see my dad.
I hadn't seen him since the day he was arrested in October of the previous year.
He was very warm.
And I asked him that-- what do we do now?
On the train already and elsewhere, there was no kosher food.
And he said, at times like this, this is excusable.
You have no choice, just be careful.
And matter of fact, there was no real kosher food.
Even though it was a Jewish home, it was not a kosher-type home.

And the other thing I must mention-- might not be that relevant, but I will mention it-- is at the train, we had checked in the bicycle that I had received for my bar mitzvah the previous year, to take with me, but they didn't bring it across the border.
And it was left behind in [?
Zweibrucken, ?] which is the border town from Germany to France.
And I was very concerned about not having the bike.
And two or three days later, my aunt, or the cousin that I mentioned, had gone to retrieve the bike, paying the duty, which had not been paid.
And I ended up with the bike in that home.
So I was the only boy in that home that had a bicycle.
I was also the only boy in that home that spoke French.
I had had almost four years of French, and I felt quite important, you know, as a 14-year-old.
I wasn't even 14.
I was 13 years old.
And I felt that hey, I had certain abilities that I was able to put to good use.
No.
He said, he said to me, did your mother say-- did your mother say anything as to when she was coming?
That's the first question he asked me.
And I said no, she had not said anything to me when she sent me off on that transport.
And the next thing he said was that he intended to stay in France as long as the family was still in Europe.
He explained to me, if he would go to Bolivia in South America, he would feel that the family would truly be disjointed.
So he decided to stay in France.
And in, I would say in June of 1939-- history will, will, you know, prove it out, things were not good in Europe.
It was pretty evident that there was unrest.
And Germany had taken Austria.
Germany had taken Czechos-- parts of Czechoslovakia, and soon after into Poland.
And that was all known.
So the war, the symptoms of war, were, were there.
And my father still felt that he could stay in France.
Well, as a matter of fact, soon I and a few other boys went to public school there in this community called Quincy-sous-Senart.
Quincy is a suburb of Paris approximately 20 miles south of Paris.
And I remember attending the school and getting an eight year-- what they give out, [FRENCH] is a school accomplished-- what am I looking for?
A diploma that demonstrates eight years of primary school, which I received within a few weeks' time, which gave me opening to go to a university, you know, to a secondary school.
And unfortunately, before that happened, the war broke out.
In September of 1939, the war broke out.
And we were all very scared.
We had heard of what happened in Poland, the kids.
And we packed up from this home of 45 kids.
We packed up a cart with some belongings-- I, with my bicycle-- and we went south of the community we were in, hoping to somehow not be caught by the Germans.
By then, there was a Maginot Line and a Siegfried Line, and we figured the Germans could not enter France.
But we did not want to be caught by them either.
Well, it so happened that, that didn't last very long.
And the Germans came, very quickly. .
We were, at that time, in a farm about 50 miles south of the town that we had left from.
And there were the German soldiers coming in.
And they said you might as well return from where you were, because this is not safe for you.
They were not behaving as, at that time, as tyrants.
They were the army.
And they had-- France had capitulated.
Right.
The army officers said go back to where you were in the children's home, and it's fine.
And we did that.
And then when we did arrive there, Germans had quartered there with their horses.
The cavalry division headquartered--
At the home we were in.
And things were pretty helter skelter.
I'm trying to remember the actual time.
I think it was May, 1940.
The war broke out in 1939, and I think France fell in May of 1940.
So this was, again, springtime.
And it was the end of our home, and it was the end of a peaceful time.
The Germans were in command.
What happened then?
The children were taken, some to various homes in Paris, homes that were controlled by the O.S.E.
And I went to a private school, along with three other of the students in Paris, to a school in the community of Clamart-- C-L-A-M-A-R-T-- very strict school.

And by the summer-- when was it by the fall of 1940-- a organization had helped the O.S.E. and our group to bring us together into a home south of the demarcation line of France, which by then had been established by the Germans.
France was divvied up-- the northern part, occupied France, and the southern part, which they called Free France, which was run by Vichy, which was run by Laval.
But since this was so early in the game in 1940, no one knew that there was going to be a concerted effort to not allow Jews to stay.
This was to come.
But we then went to this home in Chabannes.
I left the private school I had been in about five months.
And the group, plus other children that came from other parts of France, and even Europe, got together in this home called Chabannes, a community near Limoges in the Department of Creuse.
Chabannes was a village.
It had a private-- it had a small public school.
And it was a home that housed-- it was a coed home-- I would say about 180 children.
And about a third of them were girls.
And they were three groups-- the young, the middle, and the older ones.
The young were age group of 5 to 8.
The middle ones were a 9 to 12.
Then the older ones were the teens, and to 15.
So that was part of my group.
They had a school in that home, which was run by ORT, O-R-T.
And it consisted of leather work, [NON-ENGLISH], leather work which was taught by a Russian-born Jew who had lived in France quite a while.
And it was supposed to be what we could do as, as trade.
ORT, even today, believed in helping young Jewish people to acquire a trade.
That was done there.
But we also had a, like, a boarding school going on.
We had three or four different teachers where we learned various, like, math and French and literature, and some other subjects, which was not attended by everyone.
It was attended by those who were able to follow those courses.
Some of those students, I'm proud to say, we are in touch with today.

The group that formed at that time, like a family, over the two years that I was there, became a very closely-knit group as we shared studies and the learning of the leather trade, and the general being together.
I was there from-- yes, just about two years, a little longer, two years and two months.
Yes.
We were not aware of any interference from anywhere, or any pogrom, or any hassle from antisemitic activities whatsoever.
Not at that moment.
I think I was aware.
As a matter of fact, I went to visit my father, who then lived in Marseilles.
And we were aware.
But the war, as it was settled in Europe-- let me try and think.
The war was settled in that part of Europe-- OK, the Holland, Belgium, and France-- while it was going on in Russia.
It was so distant that we were not totally aware, you know, that-- like, Stalingrad and some of the other events took place during that time.
And also that the war had-- I was aware the war had broken out in the US-- Pearl Harbor, which was in 1941.
But we were not in the loop, you might say, of that kind of awareness, until it promptly hit us in a sudden way in July of 1942.
In July of '42, a police bus came with French police in their uniforms.
And it was at 4:30 in the morning.
It was still dark.
And they had a list.
And the director of the home was called.
And he was there, and he, he read the list.
And there were six names of young men on the list, including mine, and also names of three educators.
And they were called, and they were put in the bus.
And it was said that this was a contingent of a work group that was going to be put to use in parts of France.
And we would come back before the winter time.
Age.
I was--
I was-- They took all the kids over 16.
And I, in 1942, I was going on 17, although they left some behind.
I don't know what-- there were more kids my age over 16, but they were not on that list.
I really can't tell you why.
It was only six boys.
No-- no.
Anyway--
They took us on the bus, and we were sent to a transit camp, oh, about three hours away from where they picked us up.
And I remember that the three educators in that camp disappeared.
They were no longer there.
They had left.
They were able to, to flee.
They knew what was going to happen.
I did not know.
Neither did the other five kids know what was going to happen.

And then two or three days later, they sent us from there to Drancy, which is a transition camp north of Paris, which was the famous pooling camp from where all French Jews were sent to Eastern, Eastern Europe, and eastern parts of Germany, including me.
OK, I was in Drancy less than a week.
And when I first arrived there, it was helter skelter.
It was a total disorganized pooling of people, scared and ill at ease and throwing money down the toilet, and not knowing what was going to happen.
Families that were trying to cling to one another, they were being pulled apart by the German guards and the French guards.
There were Germans there, now too, because it's in northern France.
They were both French and German guards that pulled the men apart from the women.
They were no longer dealt with as families.

And cattle cars would be, would be-- the tracks were right there where cattle cars were loaded with people boarding these cattle cars-- 50, 60 people in a relatively small, narrow-gauge type cattle car.
Before I actually went, right.
The funny thing is I don't remember that part.
All I remember was the money being flushed down toilets.
It was something that just will never leave my memory.
And I don't really know why, except that I guess they couldn't put it to use, or maybe it was money they had acquired illegally, or who knows.
They didn't want the Germans to get it.
Yeah, right.
And before I knew it, I was in one of those transports.

And one of the kids with me had, in the middle of the night, in Metz, which is in Lorraine-- it's near the German border-- had left the train and ran away and was shot by the train guards that were on the trains.
He happened to have been an artist.
He was a very--
His name was Marjan.
I don't know his last name.
M-A-R-J-A-N.
He was from Czechoslovakia.
I didn't see him run away.
I just heard the shot.
And then later, they said, if any of you do that, the same thing will happen to you.
I was in one of these cattle cars.
Very crowded.
And no real facilities for hygiene.
I was in that-- I think it was two nights and two days.
I don't remember what I had.
I had some kind of a suitcase, I think, with me with some stuff.
I had no food.
Food was given to us once a day.
On the train.
People were very ill at ease on the train, and the absence of hygiene was what was most objectionable.
You know, people would have to, you know, resort to the corners of the wagon and stuff like that.
And we were uncoupled.
I understand this particular transport went on to Auschwitz.
But the car I was in was uncoupled in a place called Gogolin-- G-O-G-O-L-I-N, which happened to have been one of the camps-- fairly new.
In Germany.
In distance from Paris, I would say probably 900 miles, way east, across Germany and into Silesia, Upper Silesia, near the town of Breslau.
Two and a half days, I believe it was.
You know, the train kept on going.
It didn't really stop.
Well, when I got there--
This was in-- by that time, it was, it must have been August of 1942.
When I got there we were shown our quarters, which consisted of burlap-covered straw bags on bunks that were two tiers high, but three people between posts sleeping on these bunks.
And there was a total-- it was fairly small.
I would say that the camp-- that car, and there was some other people there already.
So if there were 45 of us, plus maybe 300 people, total, in that particular camp.
It was small.
No, just men.
And the next day we were gathered at a courtyard and instructed to follow this German-- he was a contractor-- and that he would assign jobs to us.
They were surveying a field for the construction of some kind of a factory that was nearby.
It hadn't yet been built.
So what he was doing is he was surveying this large plain of grass-- it was level-- to prepare it for construction of a factory.
And he picked me because I spoke German, although others did too.
But he picked me, and I worked alongside him that particular few weeks that I was there.
And work was hard, but it wasn't that hard.
And the temperature was pleasant.
It's just that there wasn't enough food.
The food was a watery soup and a hunk of bread, once a day--
--that was given to us.
I don't think we had utensils.
We ate the soup by drinking it out of the bowl.
And that prompted two of, two of the boys that were with me in that camp, because of the hunger-- and one of them was a bed wetter, even though he was 16-- to resort to eating food out of trash cans.
And they promptly got, both of them, got sick, and died.
That was, oh, maybe seven, eight weeks after we were in that camp.
I witnessed them dying myself.
So that left only, that left only two besides me.
And this was only a month or so after we were in the first camp.
Yes.
One, Barron [INAUDIBLE], and the other one Arnold [?

Kachinski. ?] That was part of the group that had really cemented ties in the home that I mentioned in Chabannes in central France after the first stage of the war, and because of the commonality of education and of, of, like, a family life.
And so that was my first experience of death.
But it wasn't killing.
It was just precipitated by lack of hygiene.
I was to witness killing and other types of death later in large measure.
I was there for several weeks until the onset of winter.
I remember having been transferred to another camp called Graditz, which was to the north and to the west of there, where we were transported by-- I believe it was buses.
And in Graditz, I worked a night shift in a factory that was making ammunition.
I think we started working, like, at 9:00 in the evening, and it was, you know, until at least-- it was at least 12 hours of work.
Every day.
They were terrible.
They were just terrible conditions.
Again, there was no normal food.
We had rations that were given to us once a day of a bowl of soup and a hunk of bread.
And the soup, sometimes, was lots of water.
There was hardly anything in the soup, but it was hot.
And the hygiene conditions were substandard.
And the work was quite demanding.
Well, we were pressing gun shells into, you know, with a machine into-- from powder into shells.
And the pace of the work was incredible.
And you had to produce x many, you know, x many shells, various dimensions.
No, at that point, it was pretty well automated.
We used machinery.
The strength-- the following camp after that one was where you had to use strength, And that was a coal mine where I worked-- and it was winter, also, where we were tending to a lorry.
Those are cars that you tilt sideways that were loaded with slag coming out of the coal mine.
And that was physical work, where you had to push them and undo them.
And--
That was back in Silesia.
That was in the spring of 1941, spring of 1941.
I don't quite remember the name of that particular camp.
I'm sorry, 1943.
That's correct.
I didn't get there until 1942-- 1943.
Spring of 1943.
In the coal mine-- and I did not work in the coal mine.
I worked on the top surface dealing with the slag of the coal mine.
I was there about four months.
They got progressively worse.

Conditions got worse and worse, to where people were bartering for extra food with whatever they could manage to-- like some people were dealing with cigarettes, and some people were dealing with some food that they may have stolen from a guy next to them who was sick.
And it became almost animal-like by then.
I witnessed human beings behaving substandard and uncivilized.
And all they were thinking of was food.
Stealing, stealing, beating.
And there were certain hierarchies.
There were certain people who were stronger than other people.
And if it wasn't known to the camp guards, they would take advantage of that too.
Well, I-- by then I had no more fellow inmates that I knew from this transport from France.
They-- we had separated.
And the one other surviving young gentlemen, Wolfgang [?
Blumenreich, ?] had now left.
I was to see him again later in Birkenau, but I had not seen him for-- by that time, we were apart for over a year.
But I ran into him again in Birkenau.
It was a large camp, and I don't remember its name.
But it was-- I never, in my life, thought that human beings could survive in substandards such as the, the conditions in that camp offered, or did not offer.
And yet people were not yet dying at the rate I saw them die later on.
They were still holding on.
